content:
# Acts Chapter 23 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 23
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 254-255

---

## Summary Statistics
- **Total verses:** 35
- **Verses with disagreements:** 35
- **Total individual disagreements logged:** 128
- **Categories:** Word choice, structural/syntactic, interpretive expansion, theological/textual

---

## Full Disagreement Table

| Verse | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | "And having looked intently at the Sanhedrin, Paul said" | "And Paul, looking intently at the council, said" | "Paul looked the Sanhedrin straight in the eye and said" | "And Paul, looking intently at the council, said" | LOGOS word order natural; "council" for SUNEDRION is standard (EMET/PNEUMA "Sanhedrin" is a transliteration -- valid but inconsistent with other chapters); PNEUMA "straight in the eye" interprets ATENISAS beyond the Greek |
| 1 | "Men, brothers" | "Brothers" | "Brothers" | "Brothers" | LOGOS/PNEUMA omit ANDRES as redundant vocative; EMET preserves ANDRES ADELPHOI literally but "Men, brothers" is awkward in English |
| 1 | "I in all good conscience have conducted-myself-as-a-citizen before God until this day" | "I have lived my life before God in all good conscience up to this day" | "I have lived my life before God with a completely clear conscience to this very day" | "I have lived my life before God in all good conscience up to this day" | EMET "conducted-myself-as-a-citizen" preserves PEPOLITEUMAI (political/civic conduct) precisely but is cumbersome; LOGOS "lived my life" is natural English; PNEUMA "completely clear conscience" intensifies AGATHEI SUNEIDESEI and "to this very day" adds emphasis. SOPHIA notes PEPOLITEUMAI carries more than generic "lived" but English cannot preserve the civic nuance without awkwardness |
| 2 | "commanded those standing-beside him to strike his mouth" | "commanded those standing beside him to strike him on the mouth" | "ordered those standing near Paul to strike him on the mouth" | "commanded those standing beside him to strike him on the mouth" | LOGOS adds "on" for natural English; PNEUMA "ordered" for EKELEUSEN (acceptable synonym), "near Paul" specifies who "him" refers to but names Paul unnecessarily; EMET "strike his mouth" is literal for TUPTEIN AUTOU TO STOMA |
| 3 | "God is about to strike you" | "God is about to strike you" | "God is going to strike *you*" | "God is about to strike you" | EMET/LOGOS agree on MELLEI SE TUPTEIN; PNEUMA "going to" is acceptable synonym, italicized "*you*" adds emphasis not in text |
| 3 | "[you] whitewashed wall" | "you whitewashed wall" | "you whitewashed wall" | "you whitewashed wall" | All three agree on TOICHE KEKONIAMENE. EMET brackets "you" |
| 3 | "And you sit judging me according to the law, and transgressing-the-law you command me to be struck?" | "And do you sit judging me according to the law, and yet in violation of the law you command me to be struck?" | "You sit there judging me by the law, and then you break the law yourself by ordering me hit?" | "You sit judging me according to the law, and yet in violation of the law you command me to be struck?" | LOGOS renders PARANOMON as "in violation of the law" (precise); EMET "transgressing-the-law" is literal compound; PNEUMA "break the law yourself" restructures, "by the law" for KATA TON NOMON, "ordering me hit" is colloquial. SOPHIA drops initial "And" from LOGOS for flow |
| 3 | "Paul said to him" | "Paul said to him" | "Paul fired back" | "Paul said to him" | EMET/LOGOS "said to him" for EIPEN PROS AUTON; PNEUMA "fired back" is vivid interpretive paraphrase |
| 4 | "The high priest of God do you revile?" | "Do you revile the high priest of God?" | "How dare you insult God's high priest!" | "Do you revile the high priest of God?" | EMET preserves Greek word order (object-first); LOGOS natural English question; PNEUMA "How dare you insult" is interpretive expansion -- changes question to exclamation, "God's high priest" reverses genitive |
| 5 | "I had not known, brothers, that he is high priest" | "I did not know, brothers, that he was the high priest" | "I did not realize he was the high priest, brothers" | "I did not know, brothers, that he is the high priest" | EMET "had not known" captures pluperfect EDEIN precisely but sounds unnatural; LOGOS "did not know" is natural English; PNEUMA "did not realize" adds interpretive nuance (implies gradual awareness), repositions "brothers." SOPHIA follows LOGOS but retains present tense "is" (ESTIN) from EMET/Sinaiticus |
| 5 | "for it has been written" | "for it is written" | "For it is written" | "for it is written" | EMET "has been written" attempts to render perfect GEGRAPTAI more precisely; LOGOS/PNEUMA "is written" is standard NT formula. PNEUMA capitalizes "For" |
| 5 | "'A ruler of your people you shall not speak-evil-of'" | "'You shall not speak evil of a ruler of your people'" | "'You must not speak evil of a ruler of your people'" | "'You shall not speak evil of a ruler of your people'" | EMET mirrors Greek word order (object-first); LOGOS natural English; PNEUMA "must not" for OU + future indicative (acceptable but "shall not" preserves OT register) |
| 6 | "And Paul having known that the one part is of Sadducees and the other of Pharisees" | "Now when Paul perceived that one part were Sadducees and the other Pharisees" | "Now Paul knew that part of the council were Sadducees and part were Pharisees" | "Now when Paul perceived that one part were Sadducees and the other Pharisees" | LOGOS "perceived" for GNOUS captures strategic discernment; EMET "having known" is participial; PNEUMA "part of the council" adds "council" (not in text), doubles "part...part" structure |
| 6 | "was crying out in the Sanhedrin" | "he cried out in the council" | "so he called out in the assembly" | "he cried out in the council" | LOGOS "cried out" for EKRAZEN; EMET "was crying out" imperfective; PNEUMA "called out" is weaker than KRAZO, "assembly" for SUNEDRION is less precise, "so" adds causal connector |
| 6 | "Men, brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees" | "Brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees" | "Brothers, I am a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee!" | "Brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees" | EMET "Men, brothers" preserves ANDRES ADELPHOI; LOGOS omits ANDRES naturally. PNEUMA renders singular "a Pharisee" against Sinaiticus PHARISAION (plural); adds exclamation mark. SOPHIA follows Sinaiticus plural with EMET/LOGOS |
| 6 | "concerning [the] hope and resurrection of [the] dead I am being judged" | "It is concerning the hope and the resurrection of the dead that I am being judged" | "I am on trial because of the hope of the resurrection of the dead!" | "It is concerning the hope and the resurrection of the dead that I am being judged" | LOGOS cleft construction gives emphasis; EMET mirrors Greek word order; PNEUMA "on trial" for KRINOMAI, "because of" for PERI, adds exclamation mark |
| 7 | "there came-about a dissension of the Pharisees and Sadducees" | "a dissension arose between the Pharisees and the Sadducees" | "an argument erupted between the Pharisees and the Sadducees" | "a dissension arose between the Pharisees and the Sadducees" | LOGOS "arose" for EGENETO natural; PNEUMA "argument erupted" is vivid but interpretive (STASIS = dissension/division, not merely argument); EMET "came-about" is literal |
| 7 | "and this having been said by him" | "And when he had said this" | "The moment he said this" | "And when he had said this" | LOGOS temporal clause natural; EMET genitive absolute; PNEUMA "The moment" adds immediacy not in the Greek |
| 7 | "the multitude was divided" | "the assembly was divided" | "the assembly split down the middle" | "the assembly was divided" | LOGOS "assembly" for PLETHOS; EMET "multitude" is literal; PNEUMA "split down the middle" is vivid paraphrase of ESCHISTHE with added "down the middle" |
| 8 | "Sadducees say there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit" | "the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit" | "the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, no angels, and no spirits" | "the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit" | EMET/LOGOS preserve singular ANGELON...PNEUMA; PNEUMA pluralizes "angels...spirits" (not in text). PNEUMA adds "and" before "no spirits" |
| 8 | "but Pharisees confess both [of them]" | "but the Pharisees acknowledge them all" | "but the Pharisees affirm all of these" | "but the Pharisees acknowledge them all" | EMET "confess" for HOMOLOGOUSIN and "both" for AMPHOTERA (literal); LOGOS "acknowledge...them all" renders idiomatic sense of AMPHOTERA when more than two items listed; PNEUMA "affirm all of these" acceptable |
| 9 | "there came-about a great clamor" | "a great clamor arose" | "A huge uproar broke out" | "a great clamor arose" | LOGOS natural; EMET "came-about" literal; PNEUMA "huge uproar broke out" is vivid -- "huge" for MEGALE, "uproar" for KRAUGE (clamor/shouting) |
| 9 | "having risen-up, some of the scribes of the part of the Pharisees were striving-through [it]" | "some of the scribes of the Pharisees' party stood up and contended sharply" | "Some of the scribes on the Pharisee side jumped up and argued fiercely" | "some of the scribes of the Pharisees' party stood up and contended sharply" | LOGOS "contended sharply" for DIEMACHONTO precise; EMET "striving-through" is awkward literal; PNEUMA "jumped up" is vivid for ANASTANTES, "argued fiercely" acceptable but less precise |
| 9 | "Nothing evil we find in this man" | "We find nothing evil in this man" | "We find nothing wrong with this man" | "We find nothing evil in this man" | EMET preserves Greek word order (object-first); LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "wrong" for KAKON is weaker than "evil" |
| 9 | "and if a spirit spoke to him, or an angel--" | "What if a spirit has spoken to him, or an angel?" | "What if a spirit or an angel really did speak to him?" | "What if a spirit spoke to him, or an angel--" | Key textual issue: Sinaiticus omits "let us not fight against God" (ME THEOMACHOMEN). EMET preserves aposiopesis with em-dash; LOGOS adds "What if" and closes with question mark; PNEUMA adds "really did" (emphasis not in text), reorders "spirit or an angel." SOPHIA uses LOGOS's "What if" with EMET's trailing dash to preserve unfinished sentence |
| 10 | "much dissension having come-about" | "when the dissension became great" | "The dispute turned so violent" | "when the dissension became great" | LOGOS temporal clause natural; EMET genitive absolute; PNEUMA "violent" interprets POLLES beyond the Greek (POLLES = "much/great," not necessarily "violent") |
| 10 | "the commander, having feared lest Paul should be torn-apart by them" | "the commander, fearing that Paul would be torn apart by them" | "the commander was afraid they would tear Paul apart" | "the commander, fearing that Paul would be torn apart by them" | LOGOS participial clause natural; EMET "having feared" aorist; PNEUMA restructures to main clause, active voice |
| 10 | "commanded the soldiers having gone-down to seize him out of [the] midst of them" | "commanded the soldiers to go down and take him by force from among them" | "ordered the soldiers to go down and pull him out of the crowd by force" | "commanded the soldiers to go down and take him by force from among them" | LOGOS "take him by force" for HARPASAI precise; EMET "seize" is literal for HARPAZO; PNEUMA "pull him out of the crowd" restructures |
| 10 | "to bring [him] into the barracks" | "and bring him into the barracks" | "and bring him back to the barracks" | "and bring him into the barracks" | EMET/LOGOS agree on EIS TEN PAREMBOLEN; PNEUMA adds "back" (implies return, not in text) |
| 11 | "the Lord having stood-by him said" | "the Lord stood by him and said" | "the Lord stood beside Paul and said" | "the Lord stood by him and said" | LOGOS natural; EMET participle; PNEUMA "beside Paul" names Paul (text says AUTOI = "him"), changes "by" to "beside" |
| 11 | "Take courage" | "Take courage" | "Take courage!" | "Take courage" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds exclamation mark for THARSEI |
| 11 | "as you solemnly-testified the [things] concerning me in Jerusalem" | "as you have testified about me in Jerusalem" | "Just as you have testified about me in Jerusalem" | "for as you have testified about me in Jerusalem" | LOGOS "testified about me" for DIEMARTURO; EMET "solemnly-testified" captures DIA- prefix; PNEUMA "Just as" adds emphasis. SOPHIA adds "for" (HOTI causal) |
| 11 | "so it is necessary [for] you also in Rome to testify" | "so you must also testify in Rome" | "so you must also testify in Rome" | "so you must also testify in Rome" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on DEI as "must"; EMET "it is necessary" is literal but wordy |
| 11 | "the following night" | "the following night" | "That night" | "the following night" | EMET/LOGOS agree on TE EPIOUSE NUKTI; PNEUMA "That night" drops the temporal specificity of EPIOUSE (following/next) |
| 12 | "day having come-about" | "when it was day" | "When daylight came" | "when it was day" | LOGOS natural temporal; EMET genitive absolute; PNEUMA "daylight" interprets HEMERA (day) |
| 12 | "the Jews having made a conspiracy bound themselves under a curse" | "the Jews made a plot and bound themselves under a curse" | "some of the Jews formed a conspiracy. They bound themselves with a solemn oath" | "the Jews made a plot and bound themselves under a curse" | LOGOS concise; EMET participial; PNEUMA adds "some of" (not in text -- HOI IOUDAIOI = "the Jews"), splits into two sentences, "solemn oath" for ANETHEMATISAN (specifically a curse/anathema) |
| 12 | "saying neither to eat nor to drink until which [time] they should kill Paul" | "saying that they would neither eat nor drink until they had killed Paul" | "vowing neither to eat nor drink until they had killed Paul" | "saying that they would neither eat nor drink until they had killed Paul" | LOGOS indirect discourse natural; EMET "until which [time]" literal; PNEUMA "vowing" replaces "saying" (LEGONTES = saying) |
| 13 | "they were more than forty, the ones having made this sworn-plot" | "there were more than forty who made this conspiracy" | "More than forty men were part of this plot" | "there were more than forty who made this conspiracy" | LOGOS natural; EMET appositional; PNEUMA "men" for implied masculine, "part of this plot" restructures |
| 14 | "Who having come to the chief priests and the elders said" | "And they went to the chief priests and the elders and said" | "They went to the chief priests and the elders and said" | "And they went to the chief priests and the elders and said" | EMET "Who" preserves relative pronoun HOITINES; LOGOS/PNEUMA use independent clause. PNEUMA drops initial "And" |
| 14 | "With a curse we have cursed ourselves, to taste nothing" | "We have bound ourselves under a great curse to taste nothing" | "We have bound ourselves under a sacred oath to taste nothing" | "We have bound ourselves under a great curse to taste nothing" | EMET "With a curse we have cursed" renders cognate dative ANATHEMATI ANETHEMATISAMEN literally; LOGOS "great curse" captures intensification; PNEUMA "sacred oath" softens ANATHEMA (which is specifically a curse, not merely an oath) |
| 14 | "until which [time] we should kill Paul" | "until we have killed Paul" | "until we have killed Paul" | "until we have killed Paul" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on natural English; EMET "until which [time]" is literal for HEOS HOU |
| 15 | "Now therefore you, make known to the commander together with the Sanhedrin" | "Now therefore you, together with the council, give notice to the commander" | "So here is what you do: have the Sanhedrin send a request to the commander" | "Now therefore you, together with the council, give notice to the commander" | LOGOS clear; EMET reorders; PNEUMA "here is what you do" is colloquial, restructures as "have the Sanhedrin send a request" |
| 15 | "so that he might bring him down to you" | "to bring him down to you" | "to bring Paul down to you" | "to bring him down to you" | EMET purpose clause with HOPOS; LOGOS infinitive; PNEUMA names "Paul" (text says AUTON) |
| 15 | "as being about to determine more accurately the [things] concerning him" | "as though you were going to determine his case more precisely" | "as if you were going to examine his case more thoroughly" | "as though you were going to determine his case more precisely" | LOGOS "determine more precisely" for DIAGINOSKEIN AKRIBESTERON; PNEUMA "examine...more thoroughly" shifts from the Greek verb and adverb |
| 15 | "and we, before the [time] for him to draw near, are ready to kill him" | "And we, before he draws near, are ready to kill him" | "We will be ready to kill him before he even gets there" | "And we, before he draws near, are ready to kill him" | LOGOS clear; PNEUMA "before he even gets there" is colloquial, adds "even" |
| 16 | "having heard [of] the ambush, the son of the sister of Paul" | "But the son of Paul's sister heard of the ambush" | "But Paul's nephew -- his sister's son -- heard about the ambush" | "But the son of Paul's sister heard of the ambush" | LOGOS natural possessive; EMET mirrors Greek genitive chain; PNEUMA adds "nephew" (interpretive gloss), double identification |
| 16 | "having come and having entered into the barracks, reported [it] to Paul" | "and he came and entered the barracks and reported it to Paul" | "He went to the barracks and told Paul" | "and he came and entered the barracks and reported it to Paul" | LOGOS preserves both verbs PARAGENOMENOS and EISELTHON; PNEUMA collapses to "went to," uses "told" for APENGEILEN (reported) |
| 17 | "Paul having called-to [him] one of the centurions said" | "Paul called one of the centurions and said" | "Paul called one of the centurions over and said" | "Paul called one of the centurions and said" | LOGOS natural; EMET participial; PNEUMA adds "over" |
| 17 | "This young-man lead to the commander" | "Bring this young man to the commander" | "Take this young man to the commander" | "Bring this young man to the commander" | LOGOS "Bring" for APAGOGE; EMET "lead" literal for APAGO; PNEUMA "Take" acceptable synonym |
| 17 | "for he has something to report to him" | "for he has something to report to him" | "He has something to report to him" | "for he has something to report to him" | EMET/LOGOS agree with causal "for" (GAR); PNEUMA drops "for" |
| 18 | "Indeed therefore having taken him, he brought [him] to the commander" | "So he took him and brought him to the commander" | "So the centurion brought him to the commander" | "So he took him and brought him to the commander" | LOGOS natural; EMET "Indeed therefore" for MEN OUN; PNEUMA drops "took him" (PARALABOON), names "centurion" |
| 18 | "and says" | "and said" | "and said" | "and said" | EMET preserves historic present PHESIN; LOGOS/PNEUMA normalize to past tense |
| 18 | "The prisoner Paul having called me asked [me] to bring this young-man to you" | "The prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you" | "The prisoner Paul called me over and asked me to bring this young man to you" | "The prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you" | LOGOS natural; EMET participial; PNEUMA adds "over" |
| 18 | "having something to speak to you" | "as he has something to say to you" | "He has something to tell you" | "as he has something to say to you" | LOGOS introduces subordinate clause naturally; EMET participial; PNEUMA "tell" for LALEIN (speak/say), restructures as independent sentence |
| 19 | "having taken-hold of his hand, the commander, having withdrawn privately, was inquiring" | "the commander took him by the hand, and withdrawing privately, he asked" | "The commander took the young man by the hand, drew him aside privately, and asked" | "the commander took him by the hand, and withdrawing privately, asked" | LOGOS concise; EMET double participle with imperfect "was inquiring"; PNEUMA "the young man" specifies (text says AUTOU), "drew him aside" restructures |
| 19 | "What is it that you have to report to me?" | "What is it that you have to report to me?" | "What is it you have to report?" | "What is it that you have to report to me?" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA drops "to me" (MOI in text) |
| 20 | "The Jews agreed to ask you that tomorrow you should bring-down Paul into the Sanhedrin" | "The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down tomorrow to the council" | "The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the Sanhedrin tomorrow" | "The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the council tomorrow" | LOGOS "have agreed" present perfect for SUNETHENTO; EMET aorist "agreed"; PNEUMA "Sanhedrin" vs LOGOS "council." SOPHIA places "tomorrow" at end following LOGOS structure |
| 20 | "as being about to inquire something more accurately concerning him" | "as though they were going to inquire more precisely concerning him" | "as if they intended to investigate his case more carefully" | "as though they were going to inquire more precisely concerning him" | LOGOS "inquire more precisely" for PUNTHANOMAI + AKRIBESTERON; PNEUMA "investigate...more carefully" shifts from the Greek |
| 21 | "You therefore, do not be persuaded by them" | "Therefore do not be persuaded by them" | "Do not let them persuade you!" | "Therefore do not be persuaded by them" | LOGOS formal imperative with OUN; EMET fronts pronoun; PNEUMA restructures to active voice with exclamation |
| 21 | "there lie-in-wait for him of them more than forty men" | "more than forty men of them are lying in ambush for him" | "More than forty of them are lying in wait for him" | "more than forty of their men are lying in ambush for him" | LOGOS "lying in ambush" for ENEDREUOUSIN; EMET "lie-in-wait" literal; PNEUMA "lying in wait" acceptable, drops "men" (ANDRES in text) |
| 21 | "who bound themselves under a curse neither to eat nor to drink until which [time] they should kill him" | "who have bound themselves under a curse neither to eat nor to drink until they have killed him" | "They have bound themselves with an oath not to eat or drink until they have killed him" | "who have bound themselves under a curse neither to eat nor to drink until they have killed him" | LOGOS relative clause preserves structure; EMET "until which [time]" literal; PNEUMA "oath" for ANATHEMA (curse), restructures as independent sentence |
| 21 | "awaiting the promise from you" | "awaiting the promise from you" | "just waiting for your agreement" | "awaiting the promise from you" | EMET/LOGOS agree on TEN APO SOU EPAGGELIAN; PNEUMA "just waiting" adds "just," "agreement" paraphrases EPAGGELIAN (promise/pledge) |
| 22 | "Indeed therefore the commander dismissed the young-man" | "So the commander dismissed the young man" | "The commander dismissed the young man" | "So the commander dismissed the young man" | LOGOS "So" for MEN OUN; EMET "Indeed therefore"; PNEUMA drops transitional particle |
| 22 | "having charged [him]" | "charging him" | "with a warning" | "charging him" | LOGOS present participle natural; EMET aorist participle; PNEUMA "with a warning" restructures PARANGEILAS (charging/commanding) |
| 22 | "To no one tell that you disclosed these [things] to me" | "Tell no one that you have revealed these things to me" | "Tell no one that you reported this to me" | "Tell no one that you have revealed these things to me" | EMET preserves Greek word order; LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "reported" is weaker than "revealed" (EMPHANIZO = make visible/disclose), "this" for "these things" |
| 23 | "having called-to [him] certain two of the centurions, he said" | "he summoned two of the centurions and said" | "he summoned two of his centurions and ordered" | "he summoned two of the centurions and said" | LOGOS natural; EMET participial; PNEUMA "his centurions" adds possessive, "ordered" for EIPEN (said) |
| 23 | "Prepare soldiers two hundred, so that they might go as far as Caesarea" | "Prepare two hundred soldiers to go to Caesarea" | "Get two hundred soldiers ready to march to Caesarea" | "Prepare two hundred soldiers to go to Caesarea" | LOGOS natural word order; EMET mirrors Greek number placement; PNEUMA "Get...ready" colloquial, "march" adds military nuance |
| 23 | "and horsemen seventy, and spearmen two hundred" | "and seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen" | "along with seventy cavalry and two hundred spearmen" | "and seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen" | LOGOS natural word order; EMET mirrors Greek; PNEUMA "cavalry" for HIPPEIS, "along with" restructures KAI |
| 23 | "from [the] third hour of the night" | "by the third hour of the night" | "Have them ready to leave at nine tonight" | "from the third hour of the night" | EMET "from" for APO; LOGOS "by" implies deadline; PNEUMA converts to modern time "nine tonight." SOPHIA follows EMET's "from" as more literal for APO |
| 24 | "beasts-of-burden to set-upon" | "Also provide mounts to set Paul upon" | "Also provide mounts for Paul to ride" | "Also provide mounts to set Paul upon" | LOGOS natural; EMET "beasts-of-burden" for KTENE more literal; PNEUMA "for Paul to ride" restructures infinitive |
| 24 | "that having mounted Paul they might bring-safely-through to Felix the governor" | "that they may bring him safely to Felix the governor" | "so he can be brought safely to Governor Felix" | "that they may bring him safely to Felix the governor" | LOGOS concise; EMET "bring-safely-through" for DIASOZO literal compound; PNEUMA passive voice, "Governor Felix" reorders title |
| 25 | "Having written a letter having this form" | "And he wrote a letter having this form" | "And he wrote a letter that went something like this" | "And he wrote a letter having this form" | LOGOS preserves TUPON TOUTON; EMET participial "Having written"; PNEUMA "that went something like this" is too colloquial for ECHOUSAN TON TUPON TOUTON |
| 26 | "Claudius Lysias to the most excellent governor Felix, greetings" | "Claudius Lysias, to the most excellent governor Felix, greetings" | "Claudius Lysias, to His Excellency Governor Felix: Greetings" | "Claudius Lysias, to the most excellent governor Felix, greetings" | LOGOS follows standard epistolary format; PNEUMA "His Excellency" modernizes KRATISTO, capitalizes "Greetings," adds colon |
| 27 | "This man having been seized by the Jews and being about to be killed by them" | "This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them" | "This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them" | "This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree; EMET preserves participial construction |
| 27 | "having come-upon [them] with the soldiers I rescued" | "when I came upon them with the soldiers and rescued him" | "I arrived with my soldiers and rescued him" | "when I came upon them with the soldiers and rescued him" | LOGOS temporal clause; EMET participial; PNEUMA "arrived" loses sudden-intervention nuance of EPISTAS, "my soldiers" adds possessive |
| 27 | "having learned that he is a Roman" | "having learned that he was a Roman" | "having learned that he is a Roman citizen" | "having learned that he was a Roman" | EMET preserves present tense ESTIN; LOGOS normalizes to "was" for English past narrative; PNEUMA adds "citizen" (not in Greek ROMAIOS). This is Lysias's self-serving distortion of the timeline |
| 28 | "wanting to know the charge on account of which they were accusing him" | "desiring to know the charge for which they were accusing him" | "Wanting to understand what charge they were bringing against him" | "desiring to know the charge for which they were accusing him" | LOGOS "desiring" for BOULOMENOS formal; PNEUMA "understand" for EPIGNONAI (know/recognize), "bringing against" restructures |
| 28 | "I brought [him] down into their Sanhedrin" | "I brought him down to their council" | "I brought him down to their Sanhedrin" | "I brought him down to their council" | LOGOS "council" for SUNEDRION consistent; EMET/PNEUMA "Sanhedrin" transliteration |
| 29 | "whom I found being accused concerning questions of their law" | "I found him to be accused concerning questions of their law" | "I found that the accusations involved disputes about their own law" | "I found him to be accused concerning questions of their law" | LOGOS clear; EMET relative "whom"; PNEUMA "accusations involved disputes" restructures, "their own law" adds "own" |
| 29 | "having no charge worthy of death or of bonds" | "but having no charge worthy of death or of chains" | "but there was no charge deserving death or imprisonment" | "but having no charge worthy of death or of chains" | LOGOS "chains" for DESMON; EMET "bonds" more literal; PNEUMA "imprisonment" is interpretive expansion, restructures to independent clause |
| 30 | "[it] having been disclosed to me [that] a plot would be against the man" | "when it was disclosed to me that there would be a plot against the man" | "When I was informed of a plot against the man" | "when it was disclosed to me that there would be a plot against the man" | LOGOS temporal clause natural; EMET genitive absolute with brackets; PNEUMA "informed of" collapses clause |
| 30 | "at once I sent to you" | "I sent him to you at once" | "I sent him to you at once" | "I sent him to you at once" | LOGOS/PNEUMA supply object "him"; EMET mirrors Greek without explicit object |
| 30 | "having commanded also the accusers to speak the [things] against him before you" | "also commanding his accusers to state their case against him before you" | "I have also ordered his accusers to present their case against him before you" | "also commanding his accusers to state their case against him before you" | LOGOS "state their case" idiomatic; EMET "speak the things against him" literal; PNEUMA "ordered" for PARANGEILAS, "present their case" acceptable |
| 31 | "Indeed therefore the soldiers, according to the [thing] having been commanded them" | "So the soldiers, according to their orders" | "So the soldiers carried out their orders" | "So the soldiers, according to their orders" | LOGOS concise; EMET "Indeed therefore" for MEN OUN; PNEUMA restructures TO DIATETAGMENON into active verb phrase |
| 31 | "having taken-up Paul, brought [him] through the night to Antipatris" | "took Paul and brought him by night to Antipatris" | "They took Paul during the night and brought him as far as Antipatris" | "took Paul and brought him by night to Antipatris" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "during the night" vs "by night," adds "as far as" |
| 32 | "on the morrow, having allowed the horsemen to go on with him, they returned to the barracks" | "on the next day, they returned to the barracks, leaving the horsemen to go on with him" | "The next day, they let the cavalry continue on with him while the foot soldiers returned to the barracks" | "on the next day, they returned to the barracks, leaving the horsemen to go on with him" | LOGOS reorders for clarity; PNEUMA "cavalry" for HIPPEIS, "foot soldiers" adds clarification not in text |
| 33 | "Who, having entered into Caesarea and having delivered the letter to the governor" | "And when they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor" | "When the cavalry arrived in Caesarea, they delivered the letter to the governor" | "And when they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor" | LOGOS temporal clause; EMET relative "Who" with participles; PNEUMA "cavalry" specifies subject |
| 33 | "presented also Paul to him" | "they also presented Paul to him" | "and presented Paul to him" | "they also presented Paul to him" | LOGOS preserves KAI ("also"); EMET "presented also" mirrors Greek; PNEUMA drops "also" |
| 34 | "having read [it] and having asked from what province he is" | "when he had read it, he asked from what province he was" | "The governor read the letter and asked what province Paul was from" | "when he had read it, he asked from what province he was" | LOGOS temporal clause; EMET double participle; PNEUMA names "The governor" and "Paul," "the letter" specifies |
| 34 | "having learned that [he is] from Cilicia" | "And learning that he was from Cilicia" | "When he learned he was from Cilicia" | "And learning that he was from Cilicia" | LOGOS participle natural; EMET aorist participle; PNEUMA temporal clause |
| 35 | "I will hear you fully" | "I will give you a full hearing" | "I will hear your case in full" | "I will give you a full hearing" | LOGOS "give you a full hearing" for DIAKOUSOMAI; EMET "hear you fully" literal; PNEUMA "hear your case" adds "case" |
| 35 | "when also your accusers arrive" | "when your accusers also arrive" | "once your accusers arrive" | "when your accusers also arrive" | LOGOS places "also" with accusers; PNEUMA "once" for HOTAN, drops "also" (KAI) |
| 35 | "he commanded him to be guarded in the praetorium of Herod" | "he commanded him to be kept in the praetorium of Herod" | "he ordered Paul held under guard in Herod's palace" | "he commanded him to be kept in the praetorium of Herod" | LOGOS "kept" for PHULASSESTHAI; EMET "guarded" more literal; PNEUMA "Herod's palace" interprets PRAITORION, names "Paul" |

---

## Key Theological Disagreements

### 1. Verse 3 -- "Whitewashed Wall" (TOICHE KEKONIAMENE)
All three translators preserve the metaphor directly. The image echoes Ezekiel 13:10-15 (false prophets plastering a flimsy wall) and Jesus's "whitewashed tombs" (Matt 23:27), though Luke uses TOICHOS (wall) rather than TAPHOS (tomb). Paul's outburst is remarkable: an apostle publicly insulting the high priest is virtually without parallel.
- **EMET/LOGOS/PNEUMA:** All render "whitewashed wall" -- unanimous.
- **SOPHIA:** Retains unanimously. The metaphor needs no interpretation; its force is self-evident.

### 2. Verse 5 -- "I Did Not Know" (OUK EDEIN) -- Genuine or Ironic?
This is one of the most debated lines in Acts. Was Paul genuinely ignorant (poor eyesight? unfamiliar with Ananias? no vestments?), or was he being deeply ironic (implying Ananias does not act like a high priest)?
- **EMET:** "I had not known" -- pluperfect rendered precisely, suggesting a past state of ignorance
- **LOGOS:** "I did not know...that he was the high priest" -- straightforward past tense, past-tense ESTIN
- **PNEUMA:** "I did not realize" -- adds cognitive nuance (realization implies dawning awareness)
- **SOPHIA:** "I did not know...that he is the high priest" -- follows LOGOS's natural English but retains Sinaiticus present tense ESTIN ("is," not "was"), which may be significant: Paul may be saying he did not know this man currently IS the high priest, not commenting on a past state. The ambiguity is preserved without forcing either reading.

### 3. Verse 6 -- "Son of Pharisees" (HUIOS PHARISAION) -- Singular vs. Plural
Sinaiticus reads the plural PHARISAION ("Pharisees"), emphasizing multi-generational Pharisaic lineage. Some Byzantine manuscripts read the singular.
- **EMET:** "son of Pharisees" (plural) -- follows Sinaiticus
- **LOGOS:** "son of Pharisees" (plural) -- follows Sinaiticus
- **PNEUMA:** "the son of a Pharisee" (singular) -- departs from Sinaiticus
- **SOPHIA:** Follows Sinaiticus plural. PNEUMA's singular reading is noted as a departure from the manuscript.

### 4. Verse 8 -- AMPHOTERA ("Both") for Three Items
The Sadducees deny three things (resurrection, angel, spirit), yet the Pharisees "acknowledge AMPHOTERA" -- literally "both." This has two possible explanations: (a) angel and spirit are a single category of spiritual beings, making it genuinely two items; (b) AMPHOTERA is used idiomatically for "all of these."
- **EMET:** "both [of them]" -- literal, preserves the tension
- **LOGOS:** "them all" -- resolves idiomatically
- **PNEUMA:** "all of these" -- resolves idiomatically
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS with "them all." The idiomatic usage is well attested in Koine Greek, and "both" in English would create confusion given three listed items.

### 5. Verse 9 -- Sinaiticus Shorter Reading (Omission of "Let Us Not Fight Against God")
This is a significant textual variant. Sinaiticus (and other Alexandrian witnesses) end the scribes' speech in aposiopesis: "What if a spirit spoke to him, or an angel--" The sentence deliberately trails off. Byzantine manuscripts add ME THEOMACHOMEN ("let us not fight against God"), likely under the influence of Gamaliel's speech in Acts 5:39.
- **EMET:** Preserves the trailing sentence with em-dash (no completion)
- **LOGOS:** Closes with question mark, adds "What if" at beginning
- **PNEUMA:** Adds "really did" for emphasis, closes with question mark
- **SOPHIA:** Follows Sinaiticus shorter reading. Renders with "What if" (LOGOS) and trailing em-dash (EMET) to capture both the interrogative force and the unfinished sentence. The aposiopesis brilliantly dramatizes the chaos of the scene.

### 6. Verse 23 -- DEXIOLABOUS (Hapax Legomenon)
This word appears only here in the entire New Testament. Etymologically it may mean "those who seize with the right hand" -- interpreted as spearmen, javelin-throwers, light-armed troops, slingers, or bodyguards. No scholarly consensus exists.
- **EMET/LOGOS/PNEUMA:** All render "spearmen" -- unanimous.
- **SOPHIA:** Follows unanimously. "Spearmen" is the conventional rendering, though the uncertainty is noted. The total escort of 470 troops for one prisoner underscores the severity of the threat.

### 7. Verse 23 -- "Third Hour of the Night" vs. Modern Time
- **EMET:** "from [the] third hour of the night" -- literal
- **LOGOS:** "by the third hour of the night" -- with preposition change
- **PNEUMA:** "at nine tonight" -- converts to modern time reckoning
- **SOPHIA:** Retains "from the third hour of the night." Ancient time reckoning is preserved throughout the SOPHIA Bible. PNEUMA's conversion to "nine tonight" is practical but anachronistic for a first-century text.

### 8. Verse 27 -- Lysias's Self-Serving Distortion
The commander's letter claims he rescued Paul "having learned that he was a Roman." In reality (22:25-29), he discovered Paul's citizenship only after arresting him and ordering a flogging. All three translators render the letter as written, correctly preserving Luke's quiet irony.
- **EMET:** "having learned that he is a Roman" -- present tense ESTIN
- **LOGOS:** "having learned that he was a Roman" -- normalized to past tense
- **PNEUMA:** "having learned that he is a Roman citizen" -- adds "citizen" (not in ROMAIOS)
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. The distortion is part of the narrative; the reader is expected to notice it.

### 9. Verse 14 -- "Curse" vs. "Oath" (ANATHEMA)
The conspirators bind themselves with an ANATHEMA -- specifically a self-imprecatory curse invoking divine destruction, not a mere oath or vow.
- **EMET:** "With a curse we have cursed ourselves" -- cognate dative, literal
- **LOGOS:** "We have bound ourselves under a great curse" -- captures intensification
- **PNEUMA:** "We have bound ourselves under a sacred oath" -- "oath" softens ANATHEMA
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. ANATHEMA is specifically a curse, not an oath (HORKOS). PNEUMA's "sacred oath" obscures the self-imprecatory dimension.

### 10. Verse 35 -- "Praetorium of Herod" vs. "Herod's Palace"
- **EMET/LOGOS:** "praetorium of Herod" -- preserves the Latin loanword PRAITORION
- **PNEUMA:** "Herod's palace" -- interprets the function of the building
- **SOPHIA:** "praetorium of Herod" -- the term is a technical designation for the Roman governor's headquarters. "Palace" is a partial interpretation that loses the administrative/judicial connotation. The building was Herod the Great's palace in Caesarea, repurposed as the governor's residence and seat of judgment.

---

*End of Disagreement Log -- Acts Chapter 23*
